---
title: "A Cypher Knowledge Graph of British Imperial Territorial Evolution"
subtitle: "Hybrid dataset paper and method note on knowledge graphs for historical research"
author:
  - name: Jim Clifford
    affiliation: University of Saskatchewan
    email: jim.clifford@usask.ca
date: today
abstract: |
  This paper presents a curated Cypher knowledge graph documenting the
  territorial evolution of the British Empire from the early-modern period
  through the late twentieth century, alongside an interactive Sankey-style
  visualization of colonial successions, partitions, federations, and
  independence transitions. The graph contains 751 historical territories
  (313 colonial polities and 438 princely states) connected by 1,213 typed
  relationships drawn from Wikidata-grounded entities. We argue that property
  graphs in the Cypher query language offer a productive substrate for
  historical research: they make the messy genealogies of empire — partitions,
  reorganizations, post-imperial successor states — explicit and queryable
  without forcing them into the cleaner abstractions of relational schemas.
  We also argue that grounding historical entities to Wikidata QIDs creates
  shared foundations across projects, allowing scholars to build on each
  other's work rather than reproducing baseline structures from scratch.
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    number-sections: true
  pdf:
    toc: true
    number-sections: true
bibliography: references.bib
---

# Introduction

<!--
Brief framing: what historians of empire have done computationally, why
property graphs are a useful next step, what this paper contributes.
- Position relative to existing colonial-history datasets (Crawford, Linked
  Open Data for empire, etc.)
- The argument: Cypher + Wikidata = shared, queryable foundations
- What's *not* in scope (people, events at fine grain, economic flows)
-->

The British Empire is among the most studied historical formations, but its
*structural* evolution — which polities existed when, what succeeded what,
how partitions and federations rearranged sovereignty — is surprisingly hard
to trace at a glance. Conventional historiographical narratives treat
territorial change as background; relational databases impose clean
sovereign-state schemas that fit the post-1945 international order more
comfortably than the messy genealogies of empire.

This paper presents a Cypher property-graph dataset of 751 historical
territories and 1,213 typed relationships, together with an interactive
Sankey visualization that makes the genealogies legible
(@fig-viz). The dataset is small. The argument is larger: that knowledge
graphs in Cypher, with entities grounded to Wikidata QIDs, offer a
productive *shared foundation* for historical research at this scale.

# The dataset

## Scope and coverage

<!--
- Time span: ~1580–1985
- Geographic scope: British colonial territories worldwide
- 313 colonial polities + 438 princely states
- Excluded: dependencies of dependencies, very transient occupations,
  post-1985 successor states (mostly)
-->

## Schema

<!--
Describe the node and relationship taxonomy.
- :HistoricalTerritory base label
- Specific subtypes: Colony, CrownColony, Protectorate, Dominion,
  Mandate, PrincelyState, Federation, etc.
- Relationship types: EVOLVED_INTO, PARTITIONED_INTO, MERGED_INTO,
  FEDERATED_INTO, BECAME_INDEPENDENT, ADMINISTERED_UNDER, PART_OF, etc.
- Why typed relationships matter: they distinguish *partition* (one entity
  becomes several) from *succession* (one becomes the next) from
  *administration* (one governs another concurrently)
-->

## Provenance and curation

<!--
- Original entities harvested from Wikidata via SPARQL
- Hand-curated dates, reorganization events, edge typing
- Princely-state inclusion criteria (gun-salute precedence)
- Iteration with the visualization as a curation tool
-->

# Wikidata grounding as a shared foundation

<!--
The core methodological argument.
- Every entity has a wikidata_id property (or is flagged when ungrounded)
- Why this matters: cross-project compatibility, reuse, cumulative scholarship
- Caveat: REST API is unreliable for disambiguation (the Sarnia / Walpole
  Island problem); MCP vector search worked
- Examples of where grounding helped: identifying that Northern Rhodesia
  (1911–1964) and Northern Rhodesia (post-federation 1964) need separate
  nodes despite sharing a Wikidata QID
-->

# The visualization

<!--
- Interactive Sankey diagram (D3.js)
- What it reveals that tabular data doesn't: clusters of partition events,
  the post-1945 cascade, the persistence of crown colonies into the 1980s
- Curatorial decisions encoded visually: post-imperial entities in dashed
  outline, non-British entities (Boer republics, Dutch interludes) marked
- The visualization as a *check* on the data: drift between the embedded
  data and the live graph surfaced through the reconciliation process
-->

::: {#fig-viz}
![British Empire territorial evolution Sankey](../viz/empire_evolution.html)

Sankey-style visualization of British colonial territorial evolution.
Time runs left-to-right; each bar is a polity sized by duration.
Flows trace successions, partitions, mergers, and independences.
Color encodes region. Open the HTML file directly for the interactive
version.
:::

# Discussion: Cypher knowledge graphs for historical research

<!--
The methodological claim, made carefully.
- Property graphs vs. relational: when one is better than the other
  - Relational: clean cardinalities, controlled vocabularies, statistical work
  - Property graph: emergent typology, n-ary "becoming" relations,
    exploration of paths
- Cypher specifically: pattern-matching syntax that mirrors how historians
  *think* about successions and lineages
  - Example query: "find all territories that were once Dutch then British"
  - MATCH (a)-[:EVOLVED_INTO*]->(b) — recursion is a research tool
- Limits: property graphs do not enforce schemas; the curator's discipline
  is the only thing keeping the graph coherent
- Comparison to LINCS / linked-data approaches: complementary, not competing
-->

# Reproducibility and reuse

The full dataset is in `data/britishempire_kg_export.cypher`. To load:

```bash
cypher-shell -u neo4j -p <password> -f data/britishempire_kg_export.cypher
```

Sample queries:

```cypher
-- All settlements that became independent through partition
MATCH (a:HistoricalTerritory)-[:PARTITIONED_INTO]->(b:HistoricalTerritory)
WHERE 'IndependentNation' IN labels(b)
RETURN a.canonical_name, b.canonical_name, b.established_year
ORDER BY b.established_year;

-- Lineage of any territory back to its earliest predecessor
MATCH path = (root:HistoricalTerritory)-[:EVOLVED_INTO|:PARTITIONED_INTO|:MERGED_INTO*]->
  (target:HistoricalTerritory {canonical_name: 'Dominion of Canada'})
WHERE NOT (()-[:EVOLVED_INTO|:PARTITIONED_INTO|:MERGED_INTO]->(root))
RETURN path;
```

The visualization in `viz/empire_evolution.html` is self-contained; open in any
browser.

# Limitations and future work

<!--
Honest about what's missing.
- Fine-grained boundary changes (e.g., 19th-century Indian district
  reorganizations) are absent
- Concurrent administrative arrangements (e.g., Aden Settlement vs. Aden
  Colony) are partly conflated
- Princely-state coverage is the major-states subset; full coverage of
  the ~600+ states would require a larger curation effort
- Future: extend to French / Dutch / Spanish colonial systems with shared
  Wikidata foundations; couple with the Empire Officials KG (Colonial
  Office List) to populate territories with personnel data
-->

# References {.unnumbered}

::: {#refs}
:::
